data.title: Who will be the final straw that breaks the back of the US economy?
data.description:
Author: Zhao Ying, Wall Street Insights

The US economy is standing at the crossroads of multiple risks. The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has driven up oil prices, the private credit market is under increasing pressure, the AI ​​investment boom is showing signs of overheating, coupled with the divergence in consumer confidence and the narrowing fiscal buffer, any loss of control in any of these areas could become the fuse for a systemic crisis.

... Since Iran effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz, global daily oil supply has decreased by as much as 16 million barrels, with Brent crude briefly reaching $110 and Dubai crude breaking through $150 last week. This energy price shock has compressed the available capital of Middle Eastern sovereign wealth funds, which are precisely the key funding sources for large private AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic. Meanwhile, the US private lending market is showing warning signs of rising redemption pressures and loan default rates. Former Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyd Blankfein recently warned that the unrealized assets piling up in the private market are brewing systemic risk, and that "at some point, there will inevitably be a mandatory factor or a liquidation moment." The turmoil in the private lending market is then transmitted to the broader financial markets through the banking system. Furthermore, the divergence in consumption is intensifying, with low-income groups already bearing the brunt of the pressure. The aforementioned risks are not isolated but intertwined and mutually reinforcing, with multiple crisis threads converging towards a single trigger point. Oil Prices: A Mild Decline or a Surge to $200? The Strait of Hormuz blockade is currently the most direct external shock. According to the Wall Street Journal, Michael Haigh, a commodities analyst at Societe Generale, estimates that if the strait remains blocked for another two weeks, global inventories will fall to historic lows, and Brent crude oil could reach the historical high of $146 per barrel set in 2008. Most economists currently maintain a relatively optimistic baseline forecast. Goldman Sachs chief economist Jan Hatzius estimates that if the Strait of Hormuz reopens by mid-April, the US economy will shrink by about 0.4% a year later compared to the scenario without the strait blockade, meaning slower growth but not a recession. Reasons supporting this assessment include: after adjusting for inflation, oil prices at $146 are about 33% lower than in 2008; the US economy's dependence on oil has significantly decreased; and futures markets indicate that Brent crude oil for April delivery next year will cost around $80 per barrel, with market expectations that oil prices will not remain high for long. However, tail risks cannot be ignored. Saudi Arabia currently exports approximately 4.5 million barrels of crude oil daily via the Red Sea port of Abu. The situation could escalate dramatically if Iran attacks the port or its oil pipelines, or if the Iranian-backed Houthi rebels attack ships transiting the southern Red Sea. Michael Haigh warned that in an extreme scenario, Brent crude could surge to $200 per barrel, but he also admitted, "I'm just speculating…this is unprecedented." The approximately $1.3 trillion US private lending market, along with the over $2 trillion global market, is facing its most severe stress test since its expansion cycle began. According to the Wall Street Journal, investors are uneasy about their loan exposure to high-risk sectors such as software, leading to rising redemption demand. Michael Dimler, senior vice president of private corporate credit at Morningstar DBRS, characterized the current pressure as a "normal credit downturn cycle," but acknowledged that lending performance is weakening. BlackRock has frozen redemptions for some of its consumer loan funds, and Apollo executives have publicly stated that some loans are "good if we can get 20 cents back." The transmission path is clear: Wall Street banks are providing financing to private credit funds using their loan assets as collateral. Once the default rate of the underlying loans continues to rise, banks will face collateral pressure—the recent continuous decline in bank stocks is a leading indicator. Christopher Whalen, chairman of credit research firm Whalen Global Advisors, issued a more serious warning, expressing concern about a "Lehman moment"—a chain reaction of collapse triggered by a concentrated withdrawal of funds from lenders. Richard Farley, a leveraged finance lawyer, also pointed out, "What we really need to worry about is the forced liquidation of loan portfolios, with everyone selling at the same time." Lloyd Blankfein, former CEO of Goldman Sachs, used a forest fire analogy, warning that "the longer the interval between two fires, the more tinder accumulates," and the longer the time since the last major crisis, the larger the potential for the next collapse. AI Craze: Economic Engine or Next Bubble? Artificial intelligence has been a major pillar of the US economy and stock market over the past year. Tech giants Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle are projected to spend over $2 trillion in capital over the next three years, primarily on data centers and chips. This investment wave may still provide some buffer for the economy amid the energy crisis. However, the fragility of AI investment is becoming apparent. According to the Wall Street Journal, Todd Castagno, global head of valuation, accounting, and tax at Morgan Stanley's research division, pointed out that energy and shipping restrictions caused by the Iran war will increase the difficulty of data center construction. Middle Eastern sovereign wealth funds are a significant source of capital for large private AI firms like OpenAI and Anthropic. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting the petrodollar cycle, means that hundreds of billions of dollars of Middle Eastern capital may withdraw from the AI ​​industry chain annually. Meanwhile, financing for AI infrastructure has begun to cool, and investors are increasingly concerned about capital scarcity. Castagno stated, "The capital constraints across the entire ecosystem are more severe than people imagine." A significant proportion of current AI investment relies on debt financing; if data center construction stalls, it will remove a crucial pillar supporting the economy. Consumers: How Long Can High-Income Groups Hold On? The divergence in US consumption has persisted for some time, but it hasn't completely collapsed yet. Data from Urban Institute economist Breno Braga shows that credit card delinquency rates for low- and middle-income borrowers have exceeded pre-pandemic economic peak levels. The key to supporting overall consumption lies with high-income groups—boosted by last year's stock market rally, affluent families still have a strong willingness to consume. However, this support is not solid. High-income groups are less directly sensitive to high oil prices, their cars are more fuel-efficient, and their gasoline expenditure represents a smaller proportion of their income; however, once the stock market falls sharply, the wealth effect will quickly reverse and transmit to consumption. Meanwhile, low-income groups are already on the verge of collapse. Duke University economist Matthias Kehrig estimates that a $1 increase in oil prices over the past month is equivalent to a 2% loss of income for low-income commuters, "inevitably requiring other expenditures to be sacrificed." Fiscal Buffer: Last Line of Defense or Risk Amplifier? In past economic shocks, the federal government has typically played the role of a "shock absorber," smoothing out fluctuations through increased spending, tax cuts, or bailouts of the financial system. The tax cuts passed last year are currently distributing cash to residents through higher tax refunds and lower withholding taxes, objectively creating a certain degree of unplanned buffer. But shrinking fiscal space is eroding this capacity. Annual interest payments in the US are approaching 20% ​​of fiscal revenue, the debt-to-GDP ratio is nearing a historical peak, and the Social Security fund is projected to run out by 2032. Since the start of the Iran-Iraq War, the 10-year Treasury yield has risen from below 4% to 4.32%, and last week's Treasury auctions were weaker than expected. Former White House Budget Director Mitch Daniels, who served in the Bush administration, warned: "Those who thought 'this won't happen to us,' those institutions, and even those countries, were right—until the day they were no longer right." The security of US public finances depends on both mathematics and confidence, and the reversal of confidence often comes suddenly and unexpectedly.

data.title: New possibilities for combining AI and encryption: creating a free market for "word units"
data.description: Recently, the Chinese government officially translated the term "token" as "词元" (cíyuán). For those interested in the crypto ecosystem, we all know that in the years before GPT, "token" was more frequently and widely used to refer to tokens or cryptocurrencies within the crypto ecosystem. However, the official definition now clearly does not refer to "tokens" or "cryptocurrencies" in the crypto ecosystem, but rather to the smallest unit reflecting the content analyzed by a large model. In my recollection, it seems rare for the official translation of a single word to be so swift. This demonstrates the impact of AI development and the level of importance the government places on it. In the industry, users who have truly mastered various AI tools have long been inseparable from tokens, because almost all AI tools use tokens as the unit of measurement for billing, because tokens are used to measure how much it costs to complete a task. In many well-known companies, engineers and employees in various positions have begun to use AI tools extensively to assist them in completing their work. The consumption of tokens has even become an economic cost that companies must consider. Many companies have openly included the amount of tokens they give their employees as a recruitment benefit. The more tokens an employee receives, the more powerful and efficient the tasks they can accomplish with the help of AI. What we see now is merely the reliance people have on tokens within tech companies. However, I believe that in the future, when more AI tools and applications emerge, and when AI becomes as indispensable as today's internet applications (like WeChat), everyone is likely to become as reliant on tokens as employees of large tech companies are today. This creates a strong dependence on tokens. Currently, we often hear this kind of vision: In the AI ​​era, with AI's disruption of all industries, human productivity will greatly increase, and material wealth will be extremely abundant. At that time, some material conditions that are very rare today may no longer be scarce, and their prices measured in fiat currency will be unimaginably cheap. At that time, everyone will be equal and able to live a good life. I strongly agree with the first part of this vision—that material wealth will be abundant and some material conditions will no longer be scarce. However, I am very skeptical of the second part—that everyone will be equal and able to live a good life. Because I don't believe that human society can achieve equality. This utopian fantasy has never held true in history. I always thought that back then, the differences between people might not have been certain material conditions that we value today, such as big houses and nice cars, but rather another kind of "assets" that we cannot imagine today. This is similar to how ordinary people today enjoy far better conditions in terms of food and clothing than ancient emperors and nobles; the differences between ordinary people's conditions in terms of food, clothing, and communication and those of billionaires are not significant. The differences between us and the wealthy lie in other areas, such as children's education, special medical care, and freedom of movement. So, in what areas might the differences between people lie in the future? I now imagine that the amount of "vocabulary" one possesses in the field of AI might be a crucial "asset" that differentiates people. Those with a large vocabulary can efficiently, flexibly, and extensively mobilize AI to accomplish tasks or achieve results that are simply impossible for ordinary people or those with only a limited vocabulary. Ordinary people back then might not have worried about food, clothing, or shelter, but they lacked sufficient vocabulary, so they couldn't enjoy the alternative experiences that the virtual world or AI could offer. While the importance of vocabulary is undeniable, there are still significant differences in the cost of vocabulary: primarily due to the varying cost-effectiveness of vocabulary across different large-scale models. For example, some domestically produced large-scale model units (such as MiniMax) are relatively inexpensive, while overseas large-scale model units are more expensive. However, the performance of using domestically produced large-scale model units to process tasks, especially complex tasks, is not as good as that of overseas large-scale models. Furthermore, regardless of whether they are domestically produced or overseas large-scale model units, they cannot currently be freely circulated. A senior engineer may possess far more units than he actually needs, while a dedicated enthusiast may require far more units for learning than he can be provided with. If market mechanisms can solve the issues of cost-effectiveness and free circulation of these keywords, it will greatly accelerate the use and popularization of AI. And to solve these problems, the tokens in the crypto ecosystem should play an unexpected role. Therefore, could solving the problems of free circulation and cost-effectiveness exchange of keywords be a possible application point for combining AI and Crypto?

data.title: Stablecoin Issuance Market: Four Business Models Reshaping the Market Landscape
data.description:
Author: Ryan Yoon, Source: Tiger Research, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Abstract

Stablecoin issuance is one of the most profitable businesses in the crypto industry. However, with USDT and USDC holding over 85% of the market share, it's unrealistic for new entrants to compete using the same reserve-based yield model. This report analyzes four issuers, each carving out a unique market position within the existing landscape.

Tether leads with approximately 62% market share. In addition to its core reserve-based yield model, the company is rebuilding market trust and diversifying its revenue streams through audits by the Big Four accounting firms and a $20 billion investment in new businesses.

StraitsX relies on transaction fees, rather than reserve interest, as its core revenue source. Its integration with Alipay+, GrabPay, and Visa demonstrates its real-world application value, with monthly transaction volume reaching 2.5 times its market capitalization, validating the viability of this model. The company obtained a large payment institution license from the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) before its competitors, turning regulatory authority into a competitive barrier. M0 does not directly issue stablecoins but provides shared infrastructure to help other companies issue their own stablecoins. MetaMask and Exodus have already issued stablecoins on the platform, and as issuers and developers continue to gather, network effects will further strengthen this business model. KRWQ, in the absence of a regulatory framework in South Korea, has taken the lead in entering the offshore non-deliverable forward (NDF) market for Korean won, seizing offshore demand. Once local regulations are in place, the company plans to leverage its established offshore liquidity to enter the South Korean market and replicate this model in other major non-deliverable currency markets in Asia. The stablecoin issuance market has not moved towards a single business model but rather exhibits a trend of differentiation. Different issuers, based on their own scale and market positioning, have developed distinctly different revenue strategies and are coexisting and developing. Stablecoin issuance is one of the most profitable businesses in the crypto industry, attracting an increasing number of institutional participants. Tether has taken the lead in this field, establishing an absolute advantage as a core liquidity provider in the early trading market. Following closely behind is Circle, which leverages compliance and regulation as its core advantage and went public on the NYSE in June 2025, expanding its business into the traditional financial sector. This wave of institutionalization has propelled the total market capitalization of stablecoins to approximately $300 billion, and has also prompted major jurisdictions to formally introduce regulatory frameworks. The United States signed the GENIUS Act in July 2025, establishing the first federal-level regulatory framework for payment-based stablecoins. The European Union formally implemented the Crypto Asset Markets Regulation (MiCA), and Hong Kong also enacted the Stablecoin Ordinance, marking the beginning of comprehensive regulatory competition globally. This growth momentum is expected to accelerate further. Tiger Research analysis shows that the annual net supply of stablecoins is nearly doubling, from $55 billion in 2024 to $1.01 trillion in 2025. If major jurisdictions complete relevant legislation and institutional demand truly enters the market on a large scale, even with a conservative annual growth rate of 15%, the market size is expected to exceed $600 billion by 2030. Its core profit model lies in reserve management, not issuance itself. When a user deposits $1, the issuer mints one USDT or USDC and invests that dollar in low-risk assets such as US Treasury bonds and money market funds. As issuance increases, the size of the reserves and the interest income generated will also grow. This model is essentially a game of scale. To generate substantial income through reserve interest, a circulation of hundreds of billions of dollars is needed. Currently, USDT (approximately 62%) and USDC (approximately 25%) together account for over 85% of the market share, with the remaining 15% divided among dozens of smaller issuers. For newcomers, relying solely on reserve interest to compete is not feasible. New entrants are responding by designing alternative revenue models or completely restructuring their business. Some companies integrate payment processing fees with real-economy scenarios as their core revenue source; others do not directly issue stablecoins but instead provide issuance infrastructure and earn network service fees; still others choose to first capture offshore demand in relatively less regulated currency regions, and then enter the domestic market after the regulatory framework is in place. The stablecoin issuance market has not moved towards a single model but rather exhibits a differentiated pattern. Different issuers, based on their own scale and market positioning, adopt drastically different core revenue strategies and coexist and develop. The following section will analyze the actual operation of these models based on interviews with key participants. Tether: The Market Benchmark for Stablecoins Tether was the first company to issue USDT, a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar, in 2014. Today, it holds approximately 62% of the stablecoin market share, making it an undisputed pioneer in the industry. Tether's ability to maintain its leading position over the past decade is not solely due to its first-mover advantage. What has made Tether what it is today is a series of carefully planned structural transformations: a comprehensive adjustment to its reserve asset structure, shifting from commercial paper to US Treasury bonds; the establishment of a quarterly external attestation mechanism; and a shift towards a diversified business model, reinvesting profits generated from stablecoins into artificial intelligence, energy, education, and communications. Business Model Tether's revenue sources are diverse, but reserve management is its core. **Whenever Tether issues USDT, it receives an equivalent amount in US dollars and invests in low-risk assets such as US Treasury bonds, reverse repurchase agreements, and money market funds. As issuance increases, assets under management grow in tandem, and interest income accumulates accordingly.** A portion of the reserves is also allocated to gold and Bitcoin; price increases in these two assets generate additional fair value gains. According to public information, reserve management income contributes the vast majority of its total profits. Secondary sources of income include protocol integration fees and transaction fees. In addition, Tether holds a strategic investment portfolio covering artificial intelligence, energy, and communications, independent of its USDT reserves. **Regulatory Compliance** Since the first quarter of 2025, Tether has held a stablecoin issuance license under El Salvador's Digital Assets Law and is regulated by the National Digital Assets Commission (CNAD). However, this structure is also considered lacking in transparency, and S&P gave USDT a lower transparency rating for this reason. Tether adopted a different strategy to address this challenge, namely, developing the US market separately. Under the framework of the GENIUS Act, Tether launched USAT as a separate product line tailored to the US regulatory environment, while USDT continues to be its global universal cryptocurrency product. These two markets are structurally separate, but both are being expanded simultaneously. Tether also responded to the transparency issue. While the quarterly reserve certification reports verified by BDO had previously served as the benchmark, Tether officially engaged one of the Big Four accounting firms in March 2026 to conduct a full audit of the USDT reserves. Unlike attestation that only confirms the composition of reserves at a specific point in time, a full audit examines assets, liabilities, and internal control systems to a higher standard. The market reacted accordingly. **As Tether's regulatory environment improved, Circle's stock price fell by approximately 20%, indicating that Tether has addressed its previous major competitive weaknesses and is reshaping the competitive landscape.** **Growth Strategy** Tether's growth strategy centers on RWA expansion, technological innovation, and new business development. Its flagship RWA product is Tether Gold (XAUT), a token pegged 1:1 to physical gold held in Swiss vaults. It accounts for more than half of the total market capitalization of gold-backed stablecoins, and its underlying asset size continues to expand.

New business expansion is proceeding at the same pace. Tether's proprietary portfolio exceeds $20 billion, spanning diversified sectors such as artificial intelligence, energy, media, and communications. This portfolio is completely independent of the USDT reserve and serves as a surplus capital growth engine, reinvesting profits generated from stablecoin issuance into long-term growth drivers.

Key Takeaways

Tether's case contains structural lessons that any company evaluating a stablecoin business must face.

1. Stablecoin issuance is a scalable business. Every dollar of USDT issued is invested in US Treasury bonds. As issuance increases, so does the holding of Treasury bonds, and interest income increases accordingly. Understanding this direct link between issuance and assets under management (AUM) is the starting point for any business model analysis. 2. Compliance is a prerequisite, not an option. Even Tether operates within a regulatory framework. No matter how unclear the current regulatory framework may be, business models must take regulatory integration into account from the outset. The stablecoin industry is inherently an industry operating within a regulatory framework. StraitsX: ASEAN Real Economy Stablecoin Issuer StraitsX is a Singapore-based stablecoin issuer. Its core products are XSGD (pegged to the Singapore dollar) and XUSD (pegged to the US dollar), and it has expanded to include major ASEAN currencies such as the Indonesian Rupiah (XIDR). What makes StraitsX noteworthy is not only its issuance of digital assets: it is building a payment infrastructure directly connected to the ASEAN real economy. According to data from the on-chain data platform rwa.xyz, XSGD's monthly trading volume (approximately $39.9 million) is about 2.5 times its market capitalization (approximately $15.8 million). Compared to mainstream global stablecoins such as USDT and USDC, StraitsX's absolute asset size and trading volume are still relatively small. However, its application scenarios are quite different. Mainstream stablecoins are primarily used for investment trading on cryptocurrency exchanges, while StraitsX tokens are used for everyday real-world business transactions. Data shows that the issued tokens are not idle in investors' wallets but are continuously circulating in the market. Ultimately, StraitsX is recognized as a professional payment infrastructure in the ASEAN region not only because of its on-chain metrics, but also because of its powerful B2B payment network integration. Business Model: StraitsX's revenue model is centered on payment fees. Reserve interest income is constrained by external factors such as circulation supply and interest rates, while payment fees are linked to transaction volume and therefore grow with business growth. Reserve Interest Income: The reserves corresponding to circulating XSGD and XUSD are held in trust accounts at DBS Bank, Standard Chartered Bank, and CIMB Bank. According to the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) regulations, the interest belongs to the company, not the token holders. Based on a total circulating supply of approximately $65 million, the projected annual yield is between $2.6 million and $3.25 million. Payment Fees: Fees are incurred every time a stablecoin is used for payment or settlement. Primary channels include Deposit/Withdrawal Channels (DVA), QR code payment networks (integrated with Alipay+ and GrabPay), and card issuance (sponsored by Visa BIN). Fees are tied to transaction volume, not exchange rates. OTC Trading and FX Swap Spreads: Foreign exchange spreads earned through stablecoin-to-stablecoin swaps, buying and selling transactions, and large-scale OTC transactions.

Payment fees are primarily generated through StraitsX's external network integration. Major mobile payment platforms such as Alipay+ and GrabPay, as well as global exchanges like Binance and Bybit, have adopted StraitsX's system for fund settlement, covering a variety of application scenarios.

Notably, internal data from StraitsX shows that in the past year, stablecoin payments linked to Visa cards have increased 40-fold, while the number of cards issued during the same period has increased 83-fold.

It is worth noting that internal data from StraitsX shows that in the past year, stablecoin payments linked to Visa cards have increased 40-fold, and the number of cards issued during the same period has increased 83-fold.

Regulatory Compliance The cryptocurrency industry generally believes that strict regulation restricts business expansion. StraitsX, however, takes the opposite approach, leveraging the regulatory framework of the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) as a competitive advantage. This strategy is based on StraitsX's "Major Payments Institution" (MPI) license obtained from the MAS. With this license, StraitsX is authorized to operate six of the seven major payment services regulated by the MAS. This allows the company to legally conduct cross-border remittances, foreign exchange, merchant payments, and account issuance within a single legal entity, extending far beyond simple cryptocurrency issuance. XSGD and XUSD are two stablecoins recognized as substantially compliant with the MAS Single Currency Stablecoin (SCS) regulatory framework. For institutional capital to enter the blockchain ecosystem on a large scale, bank-grade KYC and AML systems are a prerequisite. Most cryptocurrency companies operating outside the regulatory sphere fail to meet this standard. StraitsX is collaborating with regulators to develop a next-generation cryptographically-based authentication system. This move aims to meet the compliance standards required for institutional funding inflows in advance, enabling StraitsX to exclusively attract these funds. Growth Strategy After establishing a self-sufficient revenue model, the next goal is to enter new settlement markets. The primary driver of long-term growth is Real Asset (RWA) settlement. Demand for tokenized cash is expected to grow as it serves as a settlement mechanism for on-chain transactions of traditional assets such as stocks and bonds. StraitsX plans to meet institutional settlement needs by providing interoperability across multiple blockchain environments. Key Takeaways Summary The primary driver of long-term growth is RWA settlement. As traditional assets such as stocks and bonds move onto blockchains, the demand for tokenized cash as a settlement medium will also increase. StraitsX plans to meet institutional settlement needs as soon as possible by providing compatibility across multiple blockchain environments. 1. Speed ​​of issuance is more important than issuance volume. Non-USD issuers cannot achieve growth solely through issuance size. They must first ensure real-world use cases and integration into B2B settlement networks. The key metric is turnover rate, not market capitalization. 2. Regulatory compliance is a competitive advantage. StraitsX obtained its license from the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) ahead of schedule, transforming regulatory burdens into structural barriers to entry. Stablecoins are at the intersection of cryptocurrency and traditional finance, and therefore inherently belong to a regulated industry. The speed at which issuers achieve regulatory compliance and the extent of their interaction with regulators will be key factors determining competitiveness. M0: Shared Infrastructure for Stablecoin Creators and Issuers M0 provides shared infrastructure, allowing businesses and financial institutions to issue stablecoins. M0 does not directly issue stablecoins; instead, it provides the infrastructure that enables multiple developers to launch their own stablecoins on a common technological foundation. This structure aims to address two core issues. The stablecoin market is fragmented, with each issuer operating its own independent stablecoin issuance stack, making cross-currency compatibility structurally difficult. Without M0, stablecoin developers would face a "cold start" problem: they would have to build liquidity, partnerships, and network effects for their stablecoins from scratch on launch day. M0 addresses these two issues through a shared layer. Each stablecoin on the platform is built on common standards and technologies, allowing for instant sharing of existing liquidity and 1:1 exchange with all other stablecoins from the outset. Currently, stablecoins built on the M0 infrastructure include MetaMask's mUSD, Exodus' XO Cash, KAST's USDK, Noble's USDN, and Usual's UsualM, with more under development. Issuers using the M0 issuance stack include Bridge (a Stripe company), MoonPay, and 1Money. Business Model

  • Issuer: A regulated entity that holds reserves as collateral, uses M0 infrastructure to mint stablecoins, and pays a specified interest rate to the platform from a portion of the interest earned on the reserves.

  • Builder: An entity that has specific use cases and uses M0 to launch and control its own stablecoin—capturing economic laws and directly customizing the behavior of money into its products.

MetaMask's mUSD illustrates how these two roles operate in practice.

MetaMask's mUSD illustrates how these two roles operate in practice.

MetaMask designed and built its own stablecoin, mUSD, using M0 technology and applied the necessary behavioral and product layers on top of it. Bridge holds a regulatory license, manages US Treasury bonds as collateral, and fulfills its platform obligations, ultimately minting and burning mUSD as needed. These two roles are completely separate. Bridge does not own the end-user case or the product. MetaMask also never handles the collateral. However, the stablecoins that ultimately reach users can be instantly exchanged 1:1 with all other M0-based stablecoins on the network and share liquidity from the start, rather than being built from scratch. Revenue primarily comes from interest generated on government bonds held as collateral by the issuer. While collecting this interest, issuers also pay the platform a Minter interest rate (3.33% as of March 2026) on outstanding bonds. M0's current circulating supply is approximately $276 million. This figure is expected to grow as more issuers and developers adopt the platform. Regulatory Compliance M0 positions itself as a technology platform and structurally separates compliance obligations between issuers. The core of M0's stablecoin technology embeds the compliance functions required by issuers, including allowing listing, suspension, and freezing. However, the actual operation of these functions, as well as licensing, anti-money laundering, Know Your Customer (KYC), and any other regulatory obligations, remain the direct responsibility of the individual issuers. M0 provides the technical tools but does not replace regulatory responsibility. For this division of responsibility to work in practice, issuers must comply with the relevant regulations of the markets in which they operate. M0 believes the United States is the fastest-growing market for stablecoin regulation. The GENIUS Act, which took effect in July 2025, established a federal regulatory framework for stablecoins, significantly accelerating corporate adoption. As clear regulatory frameworks are established in major jurisdictions and demand for stablecoins continues to grow, M0's opportunity to establish its infrastructure as a market standard also increases. Growth Strategy M0's current priority is to increase the total amount of M0 stablecoins circulating on its platform. Since returns based on price spreads are directly proportional to circulating supply, developing a network of builders and issuers is the most critical metric at this stage. CEO Luca Prosperi stated in a public interview that network expansion is a top priority for the next two to five years. The developer ecosystem has already diversified across wallets, games, fintech, and payments, with participants including MetaMask, Exodus, Noble, Usual, and Kast. With the accelerated growth in enterprise adoption following the enactment of the GENIUS Act, now is the optimal time to expand the issuer network. The number of issuers and developers M0 can attract to its platform during this window of opportunity will determine its long-term market position. Key Takeaways: The M0 case illustrates a shift in competition in the stablecoin market: the focus has shifted from "which stablecoin has the highest circulation" to "who controls the network and infrastructure standards for issuers and builders first." 1. Rapid integration generates network effects. Based on M0's infrastructure, all stablecoin functionalities on the platform are automatically compatible, eliminating the need for repetitive integration work for each stablecoin. 2. Infrastructure value grows with market size. Not every company has the capacity to independently issue stablecoins. As more issuers join, the value of the shared infrastructure responsible for licensing, technology, and liquidity management also increases. This is why M0's structural advantages strengthen with market growth. As long as the stablecoin market is not monopolized by a few dominant companies, the value of the common infrastructure connecting multiple issuers and builders will continue to grow. The key question for the future is whether M0's shared standards can establish their position as the industry's infrastructure layer. KRWQ: Bringing the Korean Won to the Blockchain KRWQ is a stablecoin pegged to the Korean Won (KRW), launched in October 2025 by IQ in partnership with Frax. It's worth noting that South Korea currently lacks a domestic regulatory framework for Won-denominated stablecoins. KRWQ's target market is not the South Korean domestic market, but overseas markets. The Korean Won is a currency legally traded only within South Korea, but overseas investors have shown strong demand for it, seeking to hedge or speculate on Won exchange rate fluctuations. For example, foreign investors holding Samsung Electronics stock are completely exposed to fluctuations in the Korean won exchange rate. A stronger dollar means losses, while a weaker dollar means gains. Even investors seeking to hedge this risk cannot directly hedge the won's risk outside of South Korea. This has given rise to non-deliverable forwards (NDFs): contracts settled in US dollars, with the settlement amount being the difference between the contract exchange rate and the actual exchange rate, without the need for direct won conversion. Based on this structure, the Korean won NDF market has developed into one of the largest NDF markets globally in terms of trading volume. KRWQ's strategy is to first capture demand in overseas markets and then enter the domestic market once relevant South Korean regulations are in place. Entering the overseas market first, then the domestic market, is simply a reversed order. Business Model The existing non-deliverable forward (NDF) market is an over-the-counter market whose structure revolves around bilateral bank negotiations, resulting in opaque pricing and high transaction costs. Restrictions imposed by the South Korean government on offshore won trading have narrowed the pool of eligible participants and suppressed market liquidity. Settlement requires waiting until contract expiry, which inherently introduces counterparty risk. KRWQ aims to address these limitations through perpetual futures. Non-deliverable forwards (NDFs) and perpetual futures are structurally the same product.

  • There is no direct exchange of Korean won

  • Settlement is in US dollars, based on price differences

  • Used for hedging or taking directional positions against Korean won exchange rate risk

The only difference is the maturity date. Non-deliverable forwards (NDFs) have fixed expiration dates; perpetual contracts have no expiration date, operate on-chain 24/7, and offer the same functionality at a lower cost. The only difference is the expiration date. KRWQ recently launched its NDF market through EDXM International. KRWQ employs a dual-track strategy: establishing its market overseas first, and then entering the domestic market once domestic regulations are in place. KRWQ's design fully considers the stablecoin legislation currently under review by the South Korean National Assembly, aiming to become the first compliant South Korean won stablecoin. However, the legislative environment in South Korea remains complex. Regulatory uncertainty is a major obstacle to market access in the short term, but for KRWQ, it also buys time to establish an overseas liquidity advantage over its competitors. In the final phase, KRWQ plans to partner with domestic regulated banks to enable direct deposit and withdrawal of Korean won for issuance and redemption. Growth Strategy KRWQ's growth strategy consists of three phases. Offshore Capture (Current): Building a perpetual futures trading infrastructure based on KRWQ, targeting offshore institutions and DeFi protocols. Onshore Transformation: Once domestic legislation is enacted, leverage established offshore liquidity and infrastructure to enter the South Korean market. Asian Currency Replication: Besides the South Korean won, the Indian rupee, New Taiwan dollar, and Indonesian rupiah are also major Asian non-deliverable forward (NDF) currencies. These currencies share similar structural characteristics with the South Korean won: capital controls coexist with an active offshore NDF market. Key Takeaways: 1. Regulatory Absence is Not a Waiting Period, but an Opportunity. In the Asian stablecoin market, regulation is often seen as a prerequisite for entry, with most participants indefinitely waiting for relevant legislation. KRWQ holds a different view: even without domestic regulation, the offshore market structure still exists. Offshore liquidity has become a lever for entering the domestic market. 2. The Korean Won non-deliverable forward (NDF) foreign exchange market had previously operated outside the jurisdiction of South Korean domestic regulators. KRWQ was the first to meet this need. Once regulations are in place, it will leverage its existing offshore liquidity and infrastructure to enter the South Korean domestic market. Its strategy is not to wait passively, but to start from existing profit points. Where do latecomers still have a chance to break through? The stablecoin market is highly concentrated, with USDT and USDC accounting for over 85% of the total supply. For new entrants, simply copying the same reserve interest-bearing model to compete is unrealistic. However, the cases analyzed in this report show that there is more than one path to enter this market. The core principle for latecomers is to avoid direct, homogeneous competition with Tether and Circle. Winning in terms of reserve size is no longer possible, but unique advantages can be built in different areas: payment networks, issuance infrastructure, and offshore markets. As the stablecoin market continues to expand, the competitive landscape is becoming increasingly diverse. The industry has not moved towards a single model, but rather has diversified into a market structure where multiple strategies coexist. That said, the companies covered in this report are no longer simply challengers; they have become leaders in their respective segments. Learning from their strategies is valuable, but simply copying them is far from enough. The new generation of entrants must define and solve new problems beyond the territory already established by these companies. Ultimately, the companies that survive in the stablecoin issuance market are not only those with differentiated entry strategies, but also those who can implement those strategies effectively and solve the next stage of challenges during scaling. The market has long since moved beyond the stage of "who can find a new model"; now it's about who can truly make that model work.

data.title: Behind the $285 million Drift hijacking: The arrival of an era of AI-native security.
data.description:
Editor's Note: The code was flawless, but the entire team was wiped out. The Drift incident revealed the final battleground for DeF security. I see the arrival of an era of AI-native security; AI auditing is not an option, but a necessity.

Date

April 2, 2026

Location

Palo Alto · Blue Bottle Coffee

Protocol

Drift Protocol (Solana)

Report source

fenz AI Security

After the $285 million Drift was hijacked—

On the morning of April 2, 2026, University Ave remained outwardly calm, but undercurrents were surging beneath.

Silicon Valley's Alan Walker, an investor in OG and a deep thinker, along with Kai (former head of security at Anthropic, now an independent consultant) and Dr. R (founder & CEO of fenz AI, AI Native security auditor), investigated the matter immediately.

Three people at Zombie Coffee in Palo Alto, 8 AM, three cups of coffee, two hours. The following is a transcript of the conversation. I. What exactly happened with the $285 million that went viral last night? Alan Walker: Yesterday was April Fools' Day, and I thought Drift was joking. Until I saw the on-chain data—12 minutes, 31 transactions, $285 million gone. Drift's official statement on X was, "This is not an April Fools joke." I had their LP positions at the time, and half of them evaporated. Dr. R, your Fenz AI has released an incident report. From a first-principles perspective—what kind of attack was this? Dr. R · Fenz AI: Alan, our team started tracking the anomalies in on-chain data yesterday afternoon and completed a full report early this morning. The conclusion is very clear: This was not a code vulnerability attack; not a single line of code was exploited. The attacker's path was as follows: First, they spent three weeks forging a fake asset called CVT (CarbonVote Token), and used Raydium to create a $500 liquidity pool, fooling the oracle through wash trading; then, they obtained the pre-signed transactions of two signers in the multisig through social engineering; finally, they used Solana's Durable Nonce mechanism to "freeze" these signatures, and then precisely detonated them at 16:05 UTC on April 1st. The entire preparation period was 9 days, and the execution time was 12 minutes. Fenz AI's core judgment: This was a systemic collapse of the governance control plane, not a smart contract vulnerability. Both Trail of Bits and ClawSecure audited the code, but they audited the code logic—the attacker never touched the code. Kai, former ANTHROPIC security expert: Dr. R's point about Durable Nonce is crucial. When working on security at Anthropic, we have an internal framework called "authorization surface vs. execution surface"—the authorization surface and the execution surface. Traditional auditing focuses entirely on the execution surface, checking for bugs when the code runs. However, this attack occurred on the authorization surface—the attacker obtained legitimate signature authorization, and the execution surface appeared perfectly normal. The on-chain monitoring system saw two "compliant" multisig transactions, triggering no alerts.

II. 2/5 Multisig: A Leverage of $500 to Move $550 Million

Alan Walker:

When I was doing investment due diligence, this protocol's security rating was always pass—Trail of Bits, ClawSecure, are top-tier institutions.

But looking back now, is the configuration of 2/5 multisig managing $550 million TVL inherently flawed in design?

Dr.R · Fenz AI:

Starting from first principles—the design of security thresholds must correspond to the cost of attacks.

A 2/5 multisig means you only need to compromise 2 people to control the entire protocol. What is the cost of compromising 2 people? Not $285M, it might just be a few months of social engineering plus some targeted phishing. This asymmetry itself is a systemic risk. Industry best practice is 4/7, plus a 24-48 hour timelock—even if an attacker gets the signature, the community has a window to detect the anomaly and freeze assets before on-chain execution. But after Drift's multisig migration, timelock = 0. Signature submission, immediate execution.

Kai · Former ANTHROPIC Security:

Even more unusual was the multisig migration itself.

On March 27th, Drift changed its Security Council to a new 2/5 configuration—4 out of 5 signers were new addresses, leaving only 1 historical signer.

For a protocol that has been operating for many years and managing over $500 million, this signal is extremely unusual.

When I was in charge of security at Anthropic, any migration of critical permissions required a full audit by an independent security team, and there had to be a Community Disclosure window.

In the DeFi space, people are too accustomed to "governance change," completely desensitized to the most dangerous operations. The Fenz AI report points out: The possibility of internal collusion cannot be ruled out. The attack occurred only 5 days after the migration—this time window needs to be taken seriously. III. Durable Nonce: Solana's "Time Bomb" Mechanism. Alan Walker: I'm not familiar with Durable Nonce—could you explain in the simplest terms how it becomes a weapon?

Dr.R · Fenz AI:

In analogy: a normal Solana transaction is like a check—it says "today" on it, and it expires after that.

It's like a signed authorization with no expiration date—you can sign it today and cash it at the bank a month later.

Durable Nonce is like a signed authorization with no expiration date—you can sign it today and cash it at the bank a month later.

Durable Nonce The attacker did the following: The signature action occurred 9 days ago, but there were no visible anomalies on the chain. This is why the real-time monitoring system completely failed—the authorization was done off-chain, and the execution plane was clean.

Kai · Former ANTHROPIC Security:

This mechanism has a corresponding concept in the field of AI security called "deferred authorization attack"—

The authorization time and the execution time are deliberately separated, causing all monitoring systems to detect at the wrong time.

When I was at Anthropic, we designed any system involving high-privilege operations, requiring the authorization chain to be

Each authorization step must occur close to execution, and cannot be pre-stored.

DeFi's Durable Nonce is a useful feature for ordinary users, but when used on admin-privileged paths, it becomes a structural vulnerability.

IV. The Blind Spot of Traditional Auditing: The Code Works, But the Personnel Are Breached

Alan Walker:

This is what frustrates me the most—Drift did conduct audits, Trail of Bits is top-tier, and ClawSecure just completed their audit in February of this year, and they all passed.

So what exactly did the audit audit? What's the use?

Dr.R · Fenz AI:

This is the biggest misconception in the entire industry.

This is the biggest misconception in the entire industry.

The scope of traditional code auditing includes: Whether the smart contract logic meets expectations and whether there are exploitable code vulnerabilities. These auditing firms are very professional, and they are not wrong—within their audit scope, Drift is indeed secure. However, this attack occurred on a level outside the scope of any traditional audit: These are all attack surface analysis, not code auditing. Chaos Labs' Omer Goldberg aptly stated, "The attacker had god-like control." However, this god-like control didn't stem from code vulnerabilities, but from the very design of the admin key's access control scope—market creation, oracle allocation, risk parameters, withdrawal limits, and circuit breakers—all contained within a single key. This was inherently a disastrous architectural decision. Kai, former ANTHROPIC security expert: I'll use Anthropic's experience as an analogy—in AI system security, we distinguish between two layers: "model-level safety" and "system-level safety." Model-level safety means the model itself doesn't output harmful content; system-level safety means the entire deployment architecture, API permissions, and access controls aren't bypassed. If you only do model-level safety, attackers will directly attack the system layer. The same applies to DeFi: code auditing is model-level, while governance architecture security is system-level. Currently, the entire industry is practically operating without any security measures at the system-level. V. AI Native Auditing: Not Just Faster Code Scanning, But a New Security Paradigm Alan Walker: OK, so what exactly does fenz AI mean by AI native auditing? It's not just about plugging in an LLM and running the code, right? Dr.R · Fenz AI: Yes, that's a crucial question. Many "AI auditing" tools on the market simply connect GPT to Solidity code to find bugs—that's just a more expensive tool than grep; its essence is still code auditing. What we do at fenz AI is something completely different, which can be explained in three layers: [Image of a GIF image] Dr. R's description of Layer 2 reminds me of a concept used internally at Anthropic—"anomaly chain detection." Individual actions may seem harmless, but when viewed in sequence, five actions reveal a complete attack intent. This is precisely the core advantage of AI over human auditing: Humans can audit code, but they cannot continuously track abnormal behavioral sequences across hundreds of thousands of transactions in a protocol. AI can. This isn't about increased speed, but an expansion of cognitive scope. VI. The Shadow of North Korea: When State-Owned Attackers Enter DeFi Alan Walker: The report mentions DPRK attribution, which Elliptic says fits the characteristics of North Korean hackers. Is this assessment credible? If it's a state-sponsored attack, then the entire industry's threat model needs to be rewritten. Elliptic's DPRK attribution is supported by several key features: premeditated wallet layering staging, specific patterns of cross-chain money laundering, and network-level signals. As mentioned in our report, Ledger's CTO pointed out that this modus operandi is highly consistent with the 2025 Bybit $1.4B hack, which was confirmed to be the work of the Lazarus Group, the group behind DPRK. If confirmed, this would be the 18th DPRK on-chain operation tracked by Elliptic in 2026. The fundamental shift in the threat model: your adversary is no longer an anonymous hacker, but an APT group backed by state resources, patient enough to prepare for nine days, and capable of simultaneously conducting social engineering, on-chain, and cross-chain money laundering. The traditional mindset that "code without vulnerabilities is safe" is completely ineffective in the face of this level of threat.

Kai · Former ANTHROPIC Security:

At Anthropic, in our threat modeling, there's a level called "nation-state level adversary"—which means assuming your attackers have nation-state-level resources and patience.

In the field of AI security, this threat level corresponds to:

They won't attack the system directly; they'll target your supply chain, your people, and the most vulnerable point in your processes.

The attack path of Drift this time—from social engineering multisig signers to durable nonce cold storage—demonstrates nation-state-level patience and systemic approach. The DeFi industry needs to realize that you are now within the scope of this level of threat because there is enough money and enough defense. VII. Circle's $230M Silence: The Ethical Dilemma of Centralized Infrastructure Alan Walker: Another thing that has caused an uproar in the community is that ZachXBT claims over $230M of USDC was transferred to Ethereum through Circle's CCTP bridge, and Circle did not freeze it. However, Circle recently froze 16 unrelated enterprise wallets. The contrast is stark. Dr. R · Fenz AI: This is one of the most thought-provoking parts of this incident. Technically, Circle has the capability to freeze these USDCs within a 6-hour window—they have precedents, they have the tools. But they chose not to act, or acted too slowly. This touches upon the most fundamental philosophical paradox of DeFi: You rely on centralized stablecoins as the lifeblood of DeFi, but when things go wrong, can you demand that this centralized entity exercise its power to protect you? If Circle can freeze them, then USDC is not a truly "permissionless" asset; it has an on/off switch. If Circle doesn't freeze them, then $230M disappears. This dilemma has no good answer—but it reveals the most vulnerable layer in the current DeFi architecture: the centralized risk of the underlying stablecoin assets. From a system architecture perspective, this is called "trusted third-party dependency"—your decentralized system contains a third party you must trust, whose behavior is beyond your control or expectations. In AI system design, there's a principle called "minimize trusted surface"—minimizing the number and scope of authority of external entities you must trust. Using Circle USDC as the core stabilizing layer in DeFi introduces a huge trusted surface, but the industry has never seriously discussed what this means. This Drift event has forced this question to the forefront for everyone. VIII. The Endgame: Keys > Code, AI Auditing is Not an Option, It's a Must. Alan Walker: OK, as an LP and investor, what should my checklist look like when I conduct security due diligence on DeFi protocols? The traditional standard of "checking for reports from top-tier auditing firms" is clearly insufficient. Dr.R · Fenz AI: A new framework for investor security should include at least five dimensions: What fenz AI is doing is turning these five dimensions into quantifiable security scores, updated in real time, so that investors and protocol users have a security dashboard that truly reflects the current risk status. After Drift, I believe the industry will have a period of reflection, and some protocols will seriously improve their governance architecture. But I'm more worried that six months later, when this issue has cooled down, some protocols will use 2/5 multisig + zero timelock to manage the next $500M TVL. A security culture isn't built on incidents, it's built on systems—on continuous monitoring, on binding standards, and on making insecure architectures pay the price in the market. AI auditing here doesn't replace human judgment, but rather makes the "security state" visible, quantifiable, and impossible to fake. I want to screenshot and save that quote from Omer Goldberg of Chaos Labs: [Image of Omer Goldberg's quote] After today's discussion, I plan to review the admin key structure of each DeFi protocol in my portfolio. Thank you both — Kai, Dr. R, this was the most valuable hour I've heard all year.

data.title: The Deep Crisis of the US Monetary System: M2 Expansion, the Nature of Inflation, and the Path to Reform
data.description:
I. Introduction: The Monetary Nature of Inflation and Global Misinterpretations

In late March 2026, the focus of global economic discussions once again centered on oil price fluctuations triggered by geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East. The mainstream view generally holds that war leads to tight oil supplies, thereby driving up inflation. However, this interpretation ignores the fundamental nature of inflation. Inflation does not originate from external supply shocks, but is a typical monetary phenomenon. Specifically, it stems from central banks creating fictitious reserves, which in turn allows commercial banks to create credit money out of thin air through fractional-reserve banking, while governments borrow these funds and spend them on the real economy. This process deviates from a sound monetary base, ultimately manifesting as a general rise in prices.

In an ideal monetary framework, simply mentioning "inflation" is sufficient to point to an expansion of the money supply.

In reality, however, the public is led to equate inflation with the Consumer Price Index (CPI). This confusion obscures the core mechanism: monetary expansion can only occur and ultimately drive up price levels through coordination between the Treasury, the central bank, and the banking system. Historical experience has repeatedly demonstrated that supply-side factors such as rising energy prices are only temporary disturbances, while the continuous growth of the money supply is the root cause of persistent inflation. This understanding is crucial for comprehending the current US economic situation, as monetary policy is at a critical turning point. II. Definition of Money and Distribution of Power under the US Constitution The provisions of the US Constitution on money are concentrated in Article I, Sections 8 and 10, which lay the foundation for the monetary system. Article I, Section 8, authorizes Congress to mint money, regulate its value, and regulate the value of foreign coinage. Here, the term "mint" historically refers to metallic currency, i.e., gold and silver coins, rather than the printing of paper money. Congress also possessed the power to regulate value, but this power was strictly limited to maintaining monetary stability. The Constitution allowed foreign gold coins to circulate and be exchanged for U.S. gold coins at the Mint; this design reflected an early open monetary environment and remained effective until the enactment of the Fiat Currency Act in the 1860s. Article I, Section 10, imposed strict restrictions on the states: prohibiting them from minting coins, issuing notes of credit, or making anything other than gold or silver coins legal tender for debt settlement. This clause is considered the strongest monetary statement in the Constitution. It explicitly required states to recognize only gold and silver coins as legitimate means of payment and prohibited local governments from creating their own money. Overall, the Constitution pointed to a federal gold and silver standard: money creation was centralized at the Federal Mint, and states were bound by sound money. This framework aimed to prevent currency devaluation and excessive credit issuance, maintaining the stability of the economic contract. However, in reality, the monetary system has deviated significantly from the original intent of the Constitution. The current paper money system is not what the founders envisioned, but rather a product driven by crisis. Understanding this deviation is a prerequisite for evaluating any monetary reform measures. III. Historical Evolution: The Centralization Shift Spurred by the Civil War Crisis A major shift in the US monetary system occurred during the Civil War. From 1862 to 1863, Congress passed three fiat currency acts, authorizing the issuance of greenback paper money that was not convertible to gold or silver. These paper currencies were backed by government credit, not metal reserves, marking the beginning of the paper money standard. Meanwhile, the National Bank Act, passed in 1863-1864, allowed the National Bank to issue a unified national currency backed by government debt. This legislative system fundamentally changed the previous era of decentralized free banking. Before the war, the United States had a relatively free banking system: private banks issued banknotes backed by gold and silver reserves, and money creation was highly decentralized. Although there were individual bank failures, the system operated efficiently and adapted to the needs of economic growth. The Civil War, as a major crisis, became a catalyst for centralized reform. The Fiat Currency Act and the National Bank Act not only solved the wartime financing needs but also permanently reshaped the monetary power structure. The federal government gained unprecedented control over currency through these laws, laying the foundation for the later Federal Reserve System. This historical lesson is clear: crises are often used to implement long-term structural changes, which often deviate from sound monetary principles. While postwar monetary centralization stabilized public finances in the short term, it led to long-term inflationary risks and banking system vulnerabilities. Reviewing this process helps us understand that any current discussion of monetary reform must trace back to the original intent of the Constitution and historical deviations. IV. Substantive Assessment of Rumors Regarding Trump's Treasury Notes Recently, rumors have circulated in the market that President Trump may sign US Treasury notes. Some argue that this signifies a return to constitutional currency or a shift to a non-Federal Reserve Notes system. Traditional US dollar banknotes are signed by the Treasury Secretary and Treasurer; a presidential signature is indeed rare. However, from a constitutional and historical perspective, this move itself has limited significance. Without fundamental reforms, Treasury notes are merely a superficial adjustment. They cannot change the underlying mechanism of money creation. The truly effective path is to repeal the Fiat Currency Act of 1862-1863 and the National Bank Act. Once these laws are repealed, the Federal Reserve System will lose its legal basis and will be difficult to sustain. The Constitution explicitly prohibits non-gold and silver coins from being legal tender; any paper money system that deviates from metal backing or the principle of decentralization will find it difficult to return to a sound path. Some analysts have mentioned using gold to back some government bonds as a transitional step, but from a pure monetary perspective, a decentralized free banking system is more in line with the spirit of the Constitution. The pre-war era of free banking proved that decentralized, metal-backed currency can effectively constrain credit expansion. Any current bill reform, if it does not address the foundations of the Fiat Currency Act and the National Bank Act, will be unlikely to produce substantial change. Policymakers need to be wary of symbolic measures masking structural problems. V. Data Analysis of Accelerated Growth in M2 Money Supply The latest M2 data released by the Federal Reserve reveals a severe situation of monetary expansion. In January 2026, the total M2 was $22.469 trillion, rising to $22.667 trillion in February, a monthly increase of approximately 0.88%, with an annualized growth rate of 10.5%. This accelerating trend is significantly faster than in previous months, indicating that the easing of monetary policy has not diminished. M2, as a broad money indicator, covers circulating cash, demand deposits, savings deposits, money market funds, etc., comprehensively reflecting the total amount of available money in the economy. Its rapid expansion directly corresponds to the credit creation process: the Federal Reserve injects reserves, commercial banks amplify credit through the multiplier, and government spending forms final demand. Since the beginning of 2020, the money supply has experienced explosive growth, directly driving up the subsequent consumer price index. Price increases are not isolated events, but rather a lagged result of the surge in the money supply. Looking at long-term charts, the scale of monetary expansion from February 2020 to April 2022 was unprecedented, equivalent to injecting a large amount of "steroids" into the economy. This process enabled the government to borrow at extremely low costs, and commercial banks to support consumption and investment with cheap credit, ultimately transmitting to prices. If the current annualized growth rate of 10.5% continues, it will further amplify inflationary pressures. Although monthly data may have seasonal factors, continuous monitoring shows that the momentum of monetary expansion remains unchanged. Mainstream business media such as Fox Business, CNBC, and Bloomberg still focus on geopolitical events, while ignoring M2 data, highlighting a bias in their analytical framework. VI. Market Dynamics Observation: Policy Signals and Asset Price Fluctuations The market performance in March 2026 highlighted the influence of policy statements. Yesterday, the stock market initially fell, with the S&P 500 index dropping by more than 1%, and Treasury yields rising into unfavorable territory. Subsequently, Trump announced an extension of the Iran deadline to April 6, leading to a brief market rebound. However, futures data showed that this effect was limited, indicating that the market is gradually moving away from excessive reliance on single statements. In the energy market, Brent crude rose to $103.50, up 1% from the previous day; West Texas Intermediate crude approached $95.30, also up about 1%. The precious metals sector showed strength: gold prices rose to around $4,450, a daily increase of about $70, with an intraday high of $4,475 and a low of $4,369; silver returned above $69.50, rising by about $1.40. Dow Jones futures rose 124 points, while S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 futures both rose by about 0.3%. The currency market was relatively calm. The dynamics of Treasury yields are particularly crucial: the 10-year yield rose to 4.44%, a slight increase from the previous close of 4.42%; the 30-year yield reached 4.96%, up 2.5 basis points. Key psychological levels are 4.5% for the 10-year yield and 5% for the 30-year yield. A break above 5.20% would send a more serious signal about debt sustainability. Overall, market volatility reflects the dual impact of geopolitics and monetary policy, but accelerated money supply remains the fundamental factor dominating long-term trends. The rise in gold and silver prices further confirms investors' demand for inflation hedging. VII. Policy Outlook: Necessary Reforms to Return to Sound Money Faced with accelerated M2 expansion and inflationary pressures, policymakers need to move beyond short-term stimulus and focus on structural reforms. Repealing the Fiat Currency Act and the National Bank Act is a prerequisite for restoring the constitutional monetary framework. Only by repealing these laws can the Federal Reserve's monopoly be broken, and money creation return to market constraints. Sound money principles emphasize metal backing or highly decentralized issuance to prevent excessive credit issuance and price distortions. History shows that while the era of free banking presented localized risks, it generally achieved a balance between monetary stability and economic growth. Currently, the global economy is transitioning from globalization to regional economic blocs, and reducing dependence on a single supplier has become a consensus. Against this backdrop, if the United States can take the lead in promoting decentralized monetary reform, it will gain an advantage in international competition. Policymakers should closely monitor M2 trends and be wary of the long-term erosion of purchasing power by monetary expansion. Although fundamental reform faces political resistance in the short term, continued monetary expansion will exacerbate economic instability, wealth distortion, and financial vulnerability. Only by returning to the original intent of the Constitution and rebuilding a system based on gold, silver, or competing currencies can a foundation for long-term prosperity be laid. This reform path, while challenging, conforms to economic laws and historical lessons, and is the fundamental way to address the current crisis.

data.title: Circle to launch cirBTC; Coinbase receives conditional approval for trust license.
data.description:
Headlines

▌Circle to Launch cirBTC, 1:1 Peg to Bitcoin and Supporting On-Chain Verification

On April 3rd, stablecoin issuer Circle announced on the X platform the upcoming launch of cirBTC, a Bitcoin-encapsulated token. cirBTC is backed 1:1 by BTC and is verifiable on-chain. Its design aims to seamlessly integrate with Circle's infrastructure and the broader DeFi ecosystem, creating a neutral infrastructure for new on-chain applications.

▌Coinbase Announces Conditional Approval for US Trust License

On April 2nd, according to market news, Coinbase announced that it has received conditional approval for a US trust license, signifying a further step towards conducting digital asset business such as custody under federal regulatory frameworks. If finalized, the license will help strengthen its compliance service capabilities for institutional clients.

... As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $66,895.85, a 24-hour change of -1.8%; ETH price is $2,055.06, a 24-hour change of -4.0%; BNB price is $583.43, a 24-hour change of -4.5%. The price of SOL is $78.90, a 24-hour change of -3.0%; the price of DOGE is $0.09032, a 24-hour change of -2.3%; the price of XRP is $1.32, a 24-hour change of -2.3%; and the price of TRX is $0.3152, a 24-hour change of -0.2%. The WLFI price is $0.09999, with a 24-hour change of +0.3%; the HYPE price is $35.26, with a 24-hour change of -2.0%. Policy: The US CFTC and Department of Justice have filed a lawsuit against the Governor and Attorney General of Illinois, accusing them of exceeding their legal authority to regulate the prediction market. On April 3, according to market news, the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and the Department of Justice (DOJ) filed a lawsuit against the Governor and Attorney General of Illinois, accusing them of exceeding their legal authority in attempting to regulate the prediction market. The two institutions stated that Illinois' regulatory measures were invalid because the state government has no jurisdiction over federally regulated prediction markets. The lawsuit emphasizes protecting innovative financial instruments from unauthorized interference by local governments.

▌ECB Executive Board Member: Digital Euro Has "Good Momentum," Expected to Launch in July 2029

On April 2, Piero Cipollone, a member of the European Central Bank's Executive Board, stated that the digital euro has "good momentum" and is expected to launch potentially in July 2029.

"There is sufficient consensus on this project," the Italian official said on Latvian television on Wednesday. "We are on the right track, and I see good momentum on the legislative front. I see increasing interest from banks in participating in the pilot program." Coinbase CLO: Compromise on stablecoin rewards in the CLARITY Act may be reached by Friday. On April 2nd, Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paul Grewal stated that a compromise on stablecoin rewards in the CLARITY Act may be reached by Friday. The Senate Banking Committee is expected to hold hearings on the terms in the coming weeks. Ministry of Industry and Information Technology: Exploring innovative businesses such as "computing power banks" and "computing power supermarkets." April 2 (Xinhua) -- The General Office of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) issued a special action plan to empower SMEs with inclusive computing power. The plan includes innovating inclusive computing power service models. It calls for improving the SME zone on the China Computing Power Platform, the computing power resource matching zone on the China SME Service Network, and the national computing power internet service node zone (collectively referred to as the SME platform zone), promoting precise matching between SME demand and computing power resource supply, and implementing flexible payment models such as "card-time," "core-time," and token-based billing. It also explores innovative businesses such as "computing power banks" and "computing power supermarkets," supporting SMEs to deposit idle computing power resources and achieve flexible access through cross-regional and cross-cycle scheduling. Furthermore, it implements an artificial intelligence SME entrepreneurship support plan, encouraging local departments to subsidize SMEs that meet industry orientations with "computing power vouchers," "storage vouchers," and "transportation vouchers" through the SME platform zone, simplifying the acquisition and usage process. April 2nd - Iranian Army Commander-in-Chief Khatami warned on April 2nd that if the enemy attempts a ground offensive, none of their soldiers will survive. He demanded that the army maintain the utmost vigilance and suspicion, constantly monitoring the enemy's movements and actions, and implementing operational plans to counter any enemy attack at the appropriate time. (CCTV International News) On April 3, sources familiar with the matter revealed that SpaceX has raised its target valuation for its initial public offering to over $2 trillion. These sources stated that the company and its advisors are disclosing this valuation target to potential investors in preparation for meetings in the coming weeks. According to previous reports from foreign media, these so-called "trial" briefings may contain more information supporting this valuation. In February, it was reported that SpaceX's valuation after acquiring Musk's xAI was $1.25 trillion. At a $2 trillion valuation, SpaceX would surpass all companies in the S&P 500 except for Nvidia, Apple, Alphabet (Google's parent company), Microsoft, and Amazon, and would also surpass two of the other seven major US stock exchanges—Meta and Musk's own Tesla. On April 2, YZi Labs announced a strategic follow-on investment in Predict.fun following the second quarter of its EASY Residency program. Strategic additional investment was made, with Susquehanna Crypto, a leading global digital asset trading company, also participating. Predict.fun has experienced rapid growth since graduating from YZi Labs' EASY Residency incubation program, facilitating over 4 million orders and exceeding $1.8 billion in trading volume.

▌Drift adjusted its multi-signature mechanism a week before the attack and did not set a time lock

On April 2nd, Yu Xian analyzed the Drift theft incident, pointing out that a week before the attack, Drift adjusted its multi-signature mechanism to "2/5" (1 old signer + 4 new signers) and did not set a time lock. The attackers subsequently gained administrator privileges, forged CVT tokens, manipulated oracles, disabled security mechanisms, and transferred high-value assets from the liquidity pool.
